title: Chapter 24: Mom
content: The office is different after hours.

I've only ever been here during the day, when the paralegals buzz around like formatted code and the blonde wood floors catch the sun through floor-to-ceiling windows and everything feels expensive and alive. Now the paralegals are gone. The windows are black mirrors reflecting the desk lamps back at themselves. The blonde wood has gone dark as bone.

Evelyn's private office is at the end of the hall. Not the conference room where we had our first meeting — the real one. Her name on the door in sans-serif letters. HART & ASSOCIATES. She's the associate. The other partners retired, or died, or whatever happens to people who can't keep up with her.
I knock once. No answer. I open the door.

The room smells like espresso — bitter, concentrated, the ghost of six shots pulled and consumed — and old paper. That yellow legal pad smell. Pulp and wood and something almost sweet, like books in a library no one visits anymore.

She's at her desk. Legal pads fanned out like playing cards. Highlighters — yellow, orange, pink — scattered across the surface like confetti from a party no one attended. Her laptop open to something dense and formatted. Two empty espresso cups sitting next to each other like they're on a date she stood up.
She doesn't look up.
"Sit down. I need to clarify something for the brief."

That's it. That's the greeting. Not "hi." Not "you look tired." Not "I've been working all night trying to figure out how to save you from eternal corporate servitude, how are you holding up?" Just case management. Just the next item on the list.

I sit. The leather desk chair creaks under me, cool and heavy against my back. It's the kind of leather that costs more than my rent — thick, buttery, the kind that remembers the shape of whoever sits in it. It's shaped to her, I realize. The chair remembers Evelyn Hart the way I can't.

She's writing. Her pen moves across a legal pad in that angular, left-slanting handwriting I've known my whole life. Grocery lists in that handwriting. Permission slips. Birthday cards that said things like "Proud of your accomplishments" instead of "I love you."
Click. She caps the pen. Reaches for a different highlighter.
"When did you first notice the emotional impairment?"
Lawyer voice. Deposition voice. The voice that bills at eight hundred dollars an hour and is worth every cent because it never, ever breaks.
"About three weeks in," I say. "After the Terms of Service agreement was finalized and my follower count started climbing. I noticed I wasn't... responding to things the way I used to."
"Can you be more specific?"

"I stopped crying at movies. I stopped feeling anxious before performances. I stopped feeling anything when my friends talked about their problems. I could still understand that they had problems. I just couldn't make myself care."
She writes. Doesn't look up.
"Can you describe the specific sensation of empathy being removed?"

I think about this. Really think. "It wasn't like something was taken. It was more like something was turned off. Like a switch. One day I could feel the weight of other people's pain in my chest, and the next day I could see it, name it, describe it — but it was on the other side of glass. Like watching an aquarium. The fish are real. The water is real. But I'm dry."
She writes faster.
"Did you consent to any form of data extraction?"

"The Terms of Service said 'emotional data optimization for enhanced content delivery.' I thought it meant algorithmic stuff. Target audience resonance. Engagement metrics. I didn't know it meant extracting the actual capacity for emotion from my—" I stop. "From me."
"Noted. Were you informed that emotional extraction would be permanent?"
"No."
"Would you have agreed to the Terms of Service if you had been so informed?"
"Obviously not."
She writes. Click. New highlighter. The orange one. She's color-coding my life. She's color-coding my soul.

I take the complimentary water bottle from the side table and drink. It tastes like plastic and municipal supply and a faint chemical sweetness — the taste of New York water filtered through a corporation's idea of hospitality. It tastes like waiting rooms. It tastes like being a client instead of a daughter.
"Did Mephisto Media representatives at any point use the word 'love' in their communications with you?"
"They used every word. That's the problem. They used all of them and none of them meant what they were supposed to mean."
She writes. Click.
"The Schedule C deduction at 50 million followers specifies 'love.'"

She says it like she's reading a statute. Like it's a line item. A clause. A sub-paragraph in a contract that could have been about anything — shipping terms, liability limitations, force majeure — but is instead about the thing that makes human beings human.
"Can you define what love means to you?"
I open my mouth.
Close it.

I think about it. Really think. Not Wikipedia-think. Not the clinical, indexed, footnoted knowledge I have now that lets me define any emotion in three syllables and cite the neurochemistry. I try to remember what it felt like to know.
"I don't know anymore."
The pen stops.

"I used to think it was... the thing that made sunsets matter. The thing that made me call people I cared about just to hear their voices. The thing that made me stay up all night talking to someone even when I had class at 8 AM." I swallow. "I used to think it was the word for the thing that happened in my chest when someone I loved walked into a room. Like a door opening. Like the lights coming on."
She's not writing.

"Now it's a Wikipedia entry. I know the definition. I can recite the neurochemistry — dopamine, oxytocin, serotonin, vasopressin. I can tell you the evolutionary function. I can quote the poets. I just can't access the experience."

The office is quiet. The building hums — HVAC, electricity, the city settling into its bones. Evelyn's pen rests on the legal pad, uncapped, the ink starting to bleed into the yellow paper. A small imperfection in her impeccable system.
She puts the pen down.

For the first time since I walked in, she looks at me. Not at me as in at my face while she formulates the next question. At me as in at her daughter. At the twenty-two-year-old sitting in her office chair who can't feel love anymore, who is about to hit 50 million followers and lose the last piece of herself that the contract hasn't already taken.
"I should have been there."

It's not an apology. Evelyn Hart doesn't do apologies. Apologies are admissions of liability, and she's built an entire career on never admitting liability. It's a fracture. A crack in the foundation that she's been patching with billable hours and strategic silence for twenty years.
"When your grandmother died." Her voice is different. Flatter. Controlled, but the control is taking everything she has. "When you were struggling with school. When you—"

She stops. Swallows. Her jaw tightens the way it does when she's about to object in court, when she's about to tear apart an opposing counsel's argument. But there's no opposing counsel here. Just us. Just the damage.
"I told myself that giving you space was the same as giving you freedom."
"It wasn't."
"I know."

Two words. I've never heard her say them before. Not like this. Not with the weight of a decade of missed calls and birthday cards with professional language and a mother who showed up to every school event in a tailored suit and left immediately after because she had a depo at nine.
She opens the top drawer of her desk. The one she keeps locked. The one that, when I was a kid, I used to imagine held state secrets or blackmail or the names of everyone who'd ever crossed her.
She takes out a photograph.
She hands it to me.

It's me. Maybe six years old. Sitting on my grandmother's lap in the old apartment on 84th Street, the one with the radiator that clanked and the kitchen that smelled like garlic and onions and my grandmother's coconut lotion. I'm wearing a sundress with strawberries on it. My hair is in two braids that only my grandmother could get right, pulling just tight enough, securing them with the yellow elastics I liked because they looked like tiny lemons.
Evelyn is behind us. Her hand on my shoulder.
We're all smiling.

I turn the photograph over in my hands. It has weight. Not much — it's just paper and ink and chemicals on glossy stock — but it has the weight of being the only thing in this office that isn't a legal document. The only thing that isn't billable.

I look at the image. I can't feel the warmth it represents. I know what warmth is, in the abstract. I know that the memory of my grandmother's lap should trigger nociceptor modulation and a parasympathetic response and a flood of something tender and unguarded in my chest. I know the mechanism. The mechanism is offline.
But I can see it.

I can see my grandmother's arms around me, the way her chin rests on the top of my head, the way her eyes are closed because she doesn't need to look at the camera — she already has what she wants. I can see my own face, round and open and so unguarded it hurts to look at, hurts in the place where hurt used to live before they renovated it into a storage closet.
And I can see Evelyn's hand on my shoulder.
Present. Protective. There.

Her fingers are curved around the crown of my shoulder the way you'd hold something precious and breakable. The way you'd hold something you were afraid to grip too hard because it might shatter, and afraid to let go of because it might drift away. Her hand is the architecture of a feeling she could never say out loud. It's an I-love-you written in the language of touch, in the only tense she knows — the present continuous. Not "I loved you." Not "I will love you." "I am loving you. Right now. In this moment. Here."
I can't feel it. But I can read the code.
I hand the photograph back.
"Keep it," she says. "Evidence."

But we both know it's not just evidence. It's the only piece of paper in Evelyn Hart's office that proves she's capable of something beyond the adversarial system. It's the exhibit she's been hiding in a locked drawer for sixteen years because it doesn't fit her theory of the case — the theory that she's fine, that she doesn't need anything, that love is a liability she can't afford.
She puts the photograph back in the drawer. Locks it.
Picks up her pen.

The shield goes back up. I can see it happen in real time — the way her shoulders square, the way her jaw sets, the way her eyes refocus on the legal pad like it's the only thing that exists. Lawyer mode. Depo mode. The mode that kept our family afloat by billing hourly and never going home.
But there's a crack in it now. And light comes through.
"The skeleton motion will be ready by noon. I'll file it pro bono."
"You don't have to—"
"I'm your lawyer. And your mother. In that order."
She pauses. The pen hovers over the pad. Her brow twitches — the tiniest fracture in the tectonic plates of her composure.
"No. The other order."
She doesn't look up. But her left hand — the one not holding the pen — moves across the desk. Finds mine. Squeezes once.
Brief. Tight. Professional.
Like a contract being signed.

It's the most physical affection Evelyn Hart has shown me in ten years. Maybe longer. It lasts maybe two seconds. Then her hand retreats back to her side of the desk, back to the neutral territory of the legal pad, back to the world where everything is documented and nothing is discussed.
But those two seconds are enough.

I stand up. The leather chair creaks again, releasing my shape back into the air. I walk to the door. She's already writing again — the pen moving, the highlighter waiting, the brief taking shape in that angular, left-slanting handwriting.
I close the door behind me.

The hallway. The elevator — down, always down, the city pulling me back toward the street. The lobby, where the security guard glances up from his phone and then back down. The revolving door, heavy and slow.
The street.

I stand on the sidewalk. Midtown at 4 AM is a ghost town made of glass and yellow cabs. The air smells like exhaust and hot pretzels and rain that hasn't fallen yet. Somewhere a siren. Somewhere a taxi honking at nothing. The city doesn't know what's happening to me. The city doesn't know that my mother just held my hand for two seconds and it was the most significant physical contact we've had since I was fourteen and she put her arm around me at my grandmother's funeral and then took a phone call before the service was over.

I feel — not love. Not warmth. Not the door-opening, lights-coming-on sensation I used to get when someone I loved walked into a room. That's gone. That's the Schedule C deduction. That's the price of 49.8 million followers and counting.
But something adjacent.
Something structural.

Like the frame of a house that's still standing even though the rooms are empty. Like a foundation that holds even when the walls are stripped to studs and the wiring is exposed and the plaster is gone. The structure persists. The load-bearing walls don't need warmth to do their job. They just need to be there.
The foundation holds.

The foundation is Evelyn Hart, who speaks in clauses and motions and billing increments instead of "I love you." Who kept a photograph in a locked drawer for sixteen years because she didn't know how to file it under any heading she was comfortable with. Who just worked all night on an emergency skeleton motion to sue Hell — actual, literal Hell — for her daughter's soul, pro bono, no questions asked, because that's what love looks like when it's been translated into the only language she speaks.
The foundation holds.

I can't feel it. But I can read the architecture. And standing here on the sidewalk at 4 AM, New York City humming around me like a server that never sleeps, I can see the structure clearly for the first time. My mother loves me the way a building loves its occupants — by not collapsing. By holding the weight. By staying standing.
It's not enough. It's never been enough. I know that. She knows that.

But tonight, at 4 AM, with 49.8 million followers and a skeleton motion being filed by noon and my soul on the line and nothing left in my chest but the faintest echo of something that used to be a sunset — tonight, it's enough to stand on.
I walk downtown. Toward home. Toward whatever comes next.
Behind me, in an office on the 34th floor, my mother is still writing.
